narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 34, your profile also includes upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which are common among applicants. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting a 75.2% chance that you would fail, which directly led to the rejection of your law school application since the school prioritizes students expected to succeed. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school grades, where you ranked in the second decile—well below the typical fifth or sixth decile for most students—which significantly pushed the model toward forecasting bar exam failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the second decile compared to the average around the sixth, added further weight in the same concerning direction, reinforcing the risk. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA stood out as a helpful factor, nearly matching the dataset average of 3.21 and pulling the prediction somewhat toward success. These three academic ranking elements were the top drivers of the decision, with your later-year performance carrying the most influence due to its strong link to bar exam outcomes in the model's assessment. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower decile rankings in early and later school years signal a pattern that often correlates with struggles on the bar, even with your solid GPA and LSAT. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of successful applicants, it wasn't enough to offset those key academic signals. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger performance in advanced courses or retakes, perhaps retaking the LSAT to push toward the average 36.7, and seek mentorship from bar-passed alumni. Consider bridge programs or post-baccalaureate courses designed to strengthen law school readiness, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends in your record. With deliberate steps, you can reshape how models like this one view your bar exam prospects.
